items.myth: Every square matrix has a full set of real eigenvectors you can use for PCA-style orthogonal axes.
items.reality: Only matrices with enough linearly independent eigenvectors diagonalize; non-symmetric matrices may have complex eigenvalues and non-orthogonal eigenvectors. PCA works on symmetric covariance matrices specifically.
items.example: A rotation matrix in R² has complex eigenvalues and no real eigenvector directions—yet PCA on symmetric covariance always yields real orthogonal principal components.

items.myth: The largest eigenvalue always corresponds to the most important feature in your dataset.
items.reality: Eigenvalues are properties of the matrix you analyze (e.g. covariance), not raw features individually; scaling features changes eigenvalues and eigenvectors.
items.example: Doubling one feature column doubles its variance contribution and rotates principal components unless you standardize first.

items.myth: If Av ≈ λv numerically, v must be an exact eigenvector.
items.reality: Approximate equality can hold for nearly invariant subspaces or with loose tolerance; always check residual ||Av - λv|| relative to ||v||.
items.example: After 5 power-iteration steps, ||Av - λv|| may be 1e-2; after 50 steps with a gap between eigenvalues, it often drops below 1e-10.
